title: Flask Intro
content:
Flask Introduction

The Lightweight Python Framework

Flask is a micro-framework for Python based on Werkzeug and Jinja 2. It is designed to be lightweight and modular, allowing developers to choose the tools they want to use for their web applications. Unlike Django, Flask does not include a database abstraction layer, form validation, or other components that might not be needed for every project.

title: Flask Installation
content:
Flask Installation

Get Started with Flask

Installing Flask is simple using pip. It is recommended to use a virtual environment to keep your project dependencies isolated.

Terminal
# Create a virtual environment
python -m venv venv

# Activate (Windows)
venv\Scripts\activate

# Install Flask
pip install Flask

title: Hello World
content:
Hello World App

Your First Flask App

A minimal Flask application is just a few lines of code. Save this as app.py and run it.

app.py
from flask import Flask

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route("/")
def hello():
    return "Hello, Flask!"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)

title: Flask Routing
content:
Routing in Flask

Handling URLs

Routing is used to map specific URLs to functions that handle the request. This is done using the @app.route() decorator.

Routing Example
@app.route("/home")
def home():
    return "Welcome to the Home Page!"

@app.route("/about")
def about():
    return "This is the About Page."

title: Variable Rules
content:
Dynamic Routing

Variable URLs

You can add variable sections to a URL by marking sections with <variable_name>. Your function then receives the <variable_name> as a keyword argument.

Variables
@app.route("/user/<name>")
def user(name):
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

@app.route("/post/<int:post_id>")
def show_post(post_id):
    return f"Post ID: {post_id}"

title: URL Building
content:
URL Building

url_for() Function

The url_for() function is used to build a URL to a specific function. It accepts the name of the function as its first argument and any number of keyword arguments.

Building URLs
from flask import url_for

@app.route("/admin")
def admin():
    return "Hello Admin"

@app.route("/guest/<guest>")
def hello_guest(guest):
    return f"Hello {guest} as Guest"

# Usage in another route
# redirect(url_for('admin'))

title: HTTP Methods
content:
HTTP Methods

GET, POST and More

By default, a route only answers to GET requests. You can use the methods argument of the route() decorator to handle different HTTP methods.

Methods Handling
from flask import request

@app.route("/login", methods=['GET', 'POST'])
def login():
    if request.method == 'POST':
        return "Logging in..."
    else:
        return "Login Form"

title: Flask Templates
content:
Templates (Jinja2)

Rendering HTML

Flask uses the Jinja2 template engine. To render a template, you can use the render_template() method.

Templates
from flask import render_template

@app.route("/hello/<user>")
def hello_name(user):
    return render_template("hello.html", name=user)

title: Static Files
content:
Static Files

CSS, JS and Images

Dynamic web applications also need static files. These are usually CSS and JavaScript files or images. Create a folder called static in your package or next to your module.

Linking Static Files
<!-- In HTML template -->
<link rel="stylesheet" href="{{ url_for('static', filename='style.css') }}">

title: Request Object
content:
The Request Object

Accessing Input

The request object contains all the data coming from the client. You can access form data, query parameters, files, etc.

Handling Requests
from flask import request

@app.route("/search")
def search():
    query = request.args.get('q')
    return f"Searching for: {query}"

title: Cookies
content:
Flask Cookies

Setting & Getting

To access cookies you can use the cookies attribute. To set cookies you can use the set_cookie method of response objects.

Cookies Handling
from flask import make_response

@app.route("/setcookie")
def setcookie():
    resp = make_response("Cookie is set")
    resp.set_cookie("userID", "sanjith")
    return resp

title: Sessions
content:
Flask Sessions

Tracking Users

A session allows you to store information specific to a user from one request to the next. Sessions are implemented on top of cookies and sign them cryptographically.

Session Usage
from flask import session

app.secret_key = "any_random_string"

@app.route("/login")
def login():
    session["user"] = "admin"
    return "User session set"

title: Flask-SQLAlchemy
content:
Database (SQLAlchemy)

ORM for Flask

Flask-SQLAlchemy is an extension for Flask that adds support for SQLAlchemy to your application. It aims to simplify using SQLAlchemy with Flask by providing useful defaults and extra helpers.

Database Setup
from flask_sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemy

app.config['SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI'] = 'sqlite:///test.db'
db = SQLAlchemy(app)

class User(db.Model):
    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    username = db.Column(db.String(80), unique=True, nullable=False)

title: Flask Forms
content:
Flask Forms (WTForms)

Cleaner Form Handling

Flask-WTF offers a simple integration of Flask and WTForms, including CSRF, file uploads, and reCAPTCHA. It helps you manage and validate HTML forms easily.

WTForms Example
from flask_wtf import FlaskForm
from wtforms import StringField, SubmitField
from wtforms.validators import DataRequired

class MyForm(FlaskForm):
    name = StringField('Name', validators=[DataRequired()])
    submit = SubmitField('Submit')

title: Error Handling
content:
Error Handling

Custom Error Pages

You can use the @app.errorhandler() decorator to register a function to handle specific HTTP errors, such as 404 Not Found or 500 Internal Server Error.

Error Handlers
@app.errorhandler(404)
def page_not_found(e):
    return render_template('404.html'), 404

@app.errorhandler(500)
def internal_server_error(e):
    return render_template('500.html'), 500

title: File Uploads
content:
File Uploads

Handling User Files

Flask allows you to handle file uploads easily. You need to make sure the HTML form has enctype="multipart/form-data" and use request.files to access the uploaded file.

Upload Logic
from werkzeug.utils import secure_filename

@app.route('/upload', methods=['POST'])
def upload_file():
    f = request.files['the_file']
    f.save('/var/www/uploads/' + secure_filename(f.filename))
    return 'file uploaded successfully'

title: Flask Blueprints
content:
Flask Blueprints

Scaling Your App

Blueprints allow you to organize your application into smaller, reusable modules. This is essential for large-scale applications where keeping all routes in one file is unmanageable.

Blueprint Setup
from flask import Blueprint

auth = Blueprint('auth', __name__)

@auth.route('/login')
def login():
    return 'Login Page'

# In main app.py
# app.register_blueprint(auth, url_prefix='/auth')

title: Flask Summary
content:
Flask Mastery

Framework Complete

Congratulations! You have covered the fundamentals and advanced topics of Flask web development. You've learned how to create routes, handle dynamic data, render templates, manage databases, handle forms, and scale with blueprints.

  • Core Concepts: Routing, URL building, and HTTP methods.
  • Frontend: Rendering templates with Jinja2 and serving static files.
  • Data Handling: Accessing the request object, cookies, sessions, and file uploads.
  • Advanced: Database integration with SQLAlchemy, form handling with WTForms, and modularization with Blueprints.

The simplicity of Flask is its greatest strength. Now go build something amazing!
